name: Respiratory System
description: 40 illustrated MCQs on the airways, larynx, bronchial tree, alveoli and lungs.
author: MJ Technology Solutions

questions.question: Functionally, the respiratory system is divided into a conducting portion and a:
questions.explanation: The system has a conducting portion (transports air) and a respiratory portion (gas exchange).

questions.question: Which of the following belongs to the RESPIRATORY (gas-exchange) portion?
questions.explanation: The respiratory portion includes respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts and alveoli.

questions.question: The medial wall of the nasal cavity (nasal septum) is formed partly by the perpendicular plate of the:
questions.explanation: The nasal septum is formed by the perpendicular plate of the ethmoid, the vomer and septal cartilage.
questions.imageAlt: Sagittal view of the nasal cavity

questions.question: The main function of the nasal conchae is to:
options: Increase the surface area of the nasal cavity
questions.explanation: The conchae increase surface area so inspired air is warmed and humidified.

questions.question: Most of the nasal cavity is lined by respiratory mucosa consisting of:
options: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with goblet cells
questions.explanation: Respiratory mucosa is pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with mucus-secreting goblet cells.
questions.imageAlt: Respiratory mucosa: ciliated cells and goblet cells

questions.question: The olfactory mucosa of the nasal cavity is specialised to contain:
questions.explanation: The olfactory mucosa contains smell receptors.

questions.question: How many paired paranasal sinuses are there?
questions.explanation: There are four paired paranasal sinuses: maxillary, frontal, sphenoid and ethmoid.

questions.question: Inflammation of the paranasal sinuses is called:
questions.explanation: Sinusitis is inflammation of the paranasal sinuses.

questions.question: The pharynx is divided into three parts. Which is the correct set?
questions.explanation: The three parts of the pharynx are the nasopharynx, oropharynx and laryngopharynx.
questions.imageAlt: The three regions of the pharynx

questions.question: The pharyngeal tonsil is located in the:
questions.explanation: The pharyngeal tonsil is located in the nasopharynx.

questions.question: The oropharynx and laryngopharynx are both lined by:
questions.explanation: Both are lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium to withstand friction from food and air.

questions.question: The largest laryngeal cartilage, forming the 'Adam's apple', is the:
questions.explanation: The thyroid cartilage forms the Adam's apple and gives anterior attachment to the vocal folds.

questions.question: Which laryngeal cartilage is ring-shaped and made of hyaline cartilage?
questions.explanation: The cricoid cartilage is a complete ring of hyaline cartilage.

questions.question: The single fibroelastic cartilage that prevents food entering the airway during swallowing is the:
questions.explanation: The epiglottis is a fibroelastic cartilage that closes off the airway when swallowing.

questions.question: The trachea is supported by how many incomplete (C-shaped) rings of hyaline cartilage?
questions.explanation: The trachea has 16 to 20 incomplete C-shaped rings of hyaline cartilage.

questions.question: The free ends of the tracheal cartilage rings are connected posteriorly by the:
questions.explanation: The trachealis (smooth) muscle joins the free ends of the C-rings and adjusts tracheal diameter.

questions.question: The internal ridge where the trachea divides into the two primary bronchi is called the:
questions.explanation: The carina is the internal ridge formed by the most inferior tracheal cartilage at the bronchial split.

questions.question: The trachea is lined by which type of epithelium?
questions.explanation: The trachea is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium.

questions.question: Why are inhaled foreign bodies more likely to lodge in the RIGHT primary bronchus?
options: It is shorter, wider and more vertical than the left
It is longer and more horizontal
questions.explanation: The right primary bronchus is shorter, wider and more vertically oriented, so aspirated objects lodge there more easily.

questions.question: How many secondary (lobar) bronchi does the RIGHT lung have?
questions.explanation: The right lung has three secondary (lobar) bronchi; the left lung has two.

questions.question: Air-conducting tubes smaller than about 1 mm in diameter are called:
questions.explanation: Tubes smaller than 1 mm are bronchioles; the smallest terminal bronchioles are under 0.5 mm.

questions.question: As the airways become smaller toward the terminal bronchioles, the epithelium changes to:
questions.explanation: The epithelium grades from pseudostratified ciliated columnar to simple cuboidal in terminal bronchioles.

questions.question: Sympathetic stimulation of bronchial smooth muscle causes:
questions.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation relaxes bronchial smooth muscle (bronchodilation); parasympathetic causes constriction.

questions.question: Which structure serves as the transition between the conducting and respiratory portions?
questions.explanation: Respiratory bronchioles are the transition between the conducting and respiratory portions.
questions.imageAlt: Terminal and respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts and alveoli

questions.question: The tiny sac-like outpocketings where gas exchange occurs are the:
questions.explanation: Alveoli are thin-walled sacs specialised for gas exchange with pulmonary capillaries.

questions.question: Approximately how many alveoli does each lung contain?
questions.explanation: Each lung contains approximately 300–400 million alveoli.

questions.question: The respiratory membrane, across which gases diffuse, is about how thick?
questions.explanation: The respiratory membrane is only about 0.5 µm thick, easing gas diffusion.
questions.imageAlt: Gas exchange between alveoli and capillaries

questions.question: The thin, simple squamous cells that form most of the alveolar lining are:
questions.explanation: Type I (squamous) cells form the alveolar lining across which gases diffuse.

questions.question: Surfactant, which reduces surface tension in the alveoli, is secreted by:
questions.explanation: Type II cuboidal cells secrete surfactant, a detergent-like substance.

questions.question: The main role of pulmonary surfactant is to:
options: Reduce surface tension and prevent alveolar collapse during expiration
questions.explanation: Surfactant lowers surface tension, preventing alveolar walls from collapsing during expiration.

questions.question: Difficulty breathing in premature babies is largely due to a lack of:
questions.explanation: Premature babies often lack surfactant, causing breathing difficulty.

questions.question: The membrane that covers the outer surface of the lungs is the:
questions.explanation: The visceral pleura covers the lungs; the parietal pleura lines the chest wall.

questions.question: The pleural fluid in the pleural cavity functions mainly to:
options: Act as a lubricant reducing friction during breathing
questions.explanation: Serous pleural fluid lubricates the pleural surfaces, minimising friction during breathing.

questions.question: The right lung is divided into how many lobes?
questions.explanation: The right lung has three lobes (oblique and horizontal fissures); the left has two.
questions.imageAlt: Lobes and fissures of the lungs

questions.question: The deep notch on the anterior border of the LEFT lung that accommodates the heart is the:
questions.explanation: The cardiac notch on the left lung's anterior border accommodates the apex of the heart.

questions.question: The only site where structures enter or exit the lung is the:
questions.explanation: The hilum on the medial aspect is the only site of entry/exit for vessels, bronchi and nerves.

questions.question: The wide, concave base of each lung rests upon the:
questions.explanation: The concave base of each lung rests upon the muscular diaphragm.

questions.question: The structural and functional units of lung parenchyma, numbering about ten in the right lung, are the:
questions.explanation: Bronchopulmonary segments (about 10 on the right) are the structural/functional units of lung tissue.

questions.question: The right lung is located slightly more superiorly than the left because of the:
options: Liver lying below on the right side
questions.explanation: The liver on the right side pushes the right lung slightly more superiorly.

questions.question: The upper (vestibule) region of the nasal cavity, which filters large particles, is lined with:
options: Skin containing hairs and sebaceous/sweat glands
questions.explanation: The vestibule is lined with skin bearing nose hairs and sebaceous/sweat glands to filter large particles.
